author: @Howie672
text:
So if I put a long horizontal drum fan on the apex of my roof. Then on one end I have magnets on the drum end and stationary coreless coils for low drag resistance generaton.
At the other end have magnets in the drum end and have the coil plate with cores and have a linier actuator that can move it close or far from the drum.

So in light winds it’s far enough away it has no resistance but as the speed increases it gets moved closer to reduce the rpm’s and generate power.

author: @tomsimmons4482
text:
Robert, I've been watching you video for sometime, they're very interesting and understandable.

I understand the point regarding rim based generation, and whilst walking the dog had an idea...

If you took your double rotor set up, attached a rim around the blades then mounted the whole shooting match vertically in a Darwinian style it would seem that you bring a lot of good things together?  In fact, probably major item missing from most of you improvements would be gearing.  This could be added by not putting a rim on the propellors, but instead putting the gearbox on the common shaft, then bringing the geared output down below the inlets to the chamber and having a "wheel" on the shaft with the magnets on the rim.

As an aside, do you think there is anything to be gained from toroidal propellers?

author: @moletrap2640
text:
I have been following this entire generator series closely, well done and I am doing some of my own 3-D prints. I have also been experimenting with an iron core versus no iron core in the coils. There's a significant trade off to be had in power generation capacity vs high resistance to turning/spinning. It would be interesting if you would do a video on coils with iron core versus no iron core. I have also been experimenting with square versus round coils. A square coil allows a much cleaner slice of the coil cross section by the magnet and therefore a more pure sin wave and I think greater power generation. Maybe the topic of another video?

Also it may be worthwhile mentioning to your viewers that PLA will not do well in the direct sunlight. I think they should be aware of that limitation.

author: @mikedodger7898
text: Love it!!! Please consider doing this on a bicycle. Could it actually charge your ebike battery?

replies.author: @mookiemorjax
replies.text:
It could charge it some, but it would not keep up with the draw of the electric motor (and muscle power is about 5x less efficient than ebike motors anyway - look up the 1000 mpg ebike article). Since bikes are such efficient machines and there are so many losses to charge and discharge a battery, you'd be better off just pedaling to assist the motor rather than pedaling to charge the motor. 

Adding a generator to the rim would only serve to make you and your motor have to work harder to turn the wheels.

author: @universeexplorer4203
text:
Amazing content as ever and I'm in awe of the effort that you put into the channel!
One thing strikes me with "micro" generation that is my concern when trying to implement the various ideas of generation on such a small scale to allow them to be useful. This is, how do we "add" these voltages, that are often much lower than say 12v, to charge something that is fairly easily found and recycled such as old (or even new) car batteries. 
Apologies if this is a daft question but I'm no expert at all with electronics or charging circuits. My understanding is that much like a car system, to charge a 12v battery say, the alternator output voltage is around 14v.
However when we are perhaps talking about generation of a fraction of this, but being generated by numerous devices simultaneously, how would one combine those outputs to give something that could charge, albeit possibly very slowly, a battery that would be inherently useful. 
Please forgive my ignorance, but has long been an important question for me, and would be so grateful if you could address. Many thanks.

replies.author: @jima3252
replies.text: Numerous ac devices that are out of phase would need to be rectified first, then they could be connected in series to increase voltage. Put that into a capacitor connected across voltage regulator input with output connected to battery.

replies.author: @universeexplorer4203
replies.text: @jima3252  Thanks. That does make sense even given my limited knowledge. Thank you.

replies.author: @jima3252
replies.text: ​@universeexplorer4203No problem, that is a good question that needs explored.

author: @oSonine
text: I like the tall gearing and thin profile of axial flux designs. The outer edge moves faster than its center so it would generate more. The trade off is always the same though. It requires more input to start moving a heavier mass on the outer rim but would also act more like a flywheel. Good show Robert I liked this video.
